title: Determination of oxidative stress in 3-dimensional reconstructed human tissues grown at the air-liquid interface using Promega’s GSH/GSSG-Glo™ Assay kit, using single lysate preparations
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"nitdw","text":"Glutathione is an endogenous antioxidant known for its protective role in various oxidative stress-mediated diseases. It is abundantly present in all human cell types and its levels, reduced (GSH) and oxidized (GSSG) are frequently used to determine the oxidative state of biological systems (Marrocco et al., 2017). It is the ratio of these biomarkers that provide more comprehensive oxidative stress information about the tissues, as opposed to other kits measuring only one. While GSH/GSSG assay kits are available from several manufacturers, Promega’s GSH/GSSG-Glo™ Assay kit seems to be highly sensitive and can be used with a low number of cells. However, the Promega assay is designed to measure total glutathione (tGSH) and GSSG in cell lysate samples from different wells or separate 3-dimensional (3D) tissues (reconstructed human tissues grown at the air-liquid interface (ALI)) or precision-cut tissue slices, with different lysis reagents to be used for assessments of each molecule. Using this assay for 3D tissues is not cost-effective as researchers would need to double the number of tissues for the experiment just for this single oxidative stress ratio endpoint. In this protocol, we describe a simple lysis method for 3D tissues that enables the use of a single tissue for measuring both GSH and GSSG using the GSH/GSSG-Glo™ Assay. The 3D tissues can be lysed using sulfosalicylic acid and then the lysate supernatants can be used immediately or stored for use at a later time. Before running the Promega assay, the samples must be neutralized using HEPES buffer and diluted as necessary to get the signal within a linear range. The samples can then be used for measuring the tGSH and GSSG using Promega’s assay procedure. The entire procedure can be completed within 5 hours.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":1303,"length":5},{"style":"italic","offset":1303,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"viuuq","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

Refrigerated centrifuge with a rotor for microcentrifuge tubes","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"hvwe2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Add 100 µL of cold 5% SSA to the apical surface of the tissue and wait for approximately 5 minutes to allow the proteins to precipitate. Note: The volume of SSA is determined based on the size of the tissue. For a 3D tissue with a surface area of approximately 1.12 cm², 100 µL has been sufficient.

Scrape the tissue using a cell scraper and transfer the resulting suspension to a microcentrifuge tube. Note: If there are cells present on the basolateral surface of the tissue insert membrane, add volume (25 µL) of the 5% SSA to the basolateral side, scrape the cells, and collect the resulting suspension.
Sonicate the sample tubes for 5 to 10 minutes in an ultrasonic ice-cold water bath.
Centrifuge the sample tubes for 10 minutes at 14000 × g and 4 °C.

Collect and aliquot the supernatants as needed. Use the samples in the GSH/GSSG assay or store them at ≤ -80 °C for later use. Note: Keep the samples under cold conditions throughout processing. Note: The lysis procedure can also be applied to other 3D tissues like precision-cut tissue slices with adjustment in reagent volumes based on the size of the tissues.
If using frozen samples, thaw the samples on ice or at 2 to 8 °C.
Neutralize the samples with an equal volume of cold neutralization buffer.

If necessary, dilute the samples further with a cold dilution buffer. Note: Use the samples to determine both tGSH and GSSG. Generally, samples for tGSH measurement need to be diluted. After neutralization and dilution, use the samples immediately in the assay as GSH may rapidly oxidize to GSSG at a neutral pH.

Prepare the GSH standards by serially diluting the glutathione stock (provided with the kit) in a buffer containing 5% SSA, neutralization buffer, and/or dilution buffer. Note: The ratio of SSA, neutralization buffer, and dilution buffer should be the same for samples and standards. The GSH standards are used to identify the linear range for measurement but are not needed for GSH/GSSG ratio analysis.
The rest of the steps are based on the kit manufacturer’s assay procedure for ‘Cells in Suspension’.
Load the samples (25 µL) and GSH standards (25 µL) on an opaque white 96-well plate.

Add 25 µL of either Total Glutathione Lysis Reagent or Oxidized Glutathione Lysis Reagent to appropriate wells designated for tGSH or GSSG determination, respectively. Note: The GSH standards should receive the Total Glutathione Lysis Reagent.
Shake for 5 to 10 minutes on a plate shaker.
Add 50 µL of Luciferin Generation Reagent to all wells and incubate at room temperature for 30 minutes.
Add 100 µL of Luciferin Detection Reagent to all wells and incubate at room temperature for 30 minutes.
Measure luminescence using a Luminometer, and calculate GSH/GSSG ratio mathematically.
The entire procedure can be completed within 5 hours.

Figure 1. Figure 1. Oxidative stress measurement in reconstructed human airway tissues and human precision-cut lung slices. The human tissues, reconstructed airways ( A ; N = 1/group) or precision-cut slices ( B ; N = 4/group) were treated with either vehicle (culture medium), hydrogen peroxide (250, 500, or 1000 µM), or menadione (100 µM) for 1 hour. Following treatment, the tissues were lysed and the lysate supernatants were stored frozen until measurement of GSH and GSSG using GSH/GSSG-Glo™ kit. The data are presented as ratios of GSH/GSSG using relative luminescence unit values, as a marker of oxidative stress.
